speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. I see you're currently on Lisinopril 20mg once daily for blood pressure control—started back on May 15th.

speaker: doctor
text: Your primary diagnosis remains essential hypertension, which we've been managing with that medication.

speaker: doctor
text: At your visit today, your heart rate was elevated at 130 beats per minute—higher than expected for resting state.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level came back at 171 g/L, which is within the upper end of normal for adult males.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve been taking the Lisinopril regularly. But over the past two days, I’ve noticed my vision getting blurry—especially when reading or looking at screens.

speaker: patient
text: It’s been pretty consistent since yesterday morning.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s concerning—your fingerstick blood glucose just came back at 12.7 mmol/L, which is significantly elevated and could explain the blurred vision.

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature is normal at 36.9°C, so no signs of acute infection right now.

speaker: patient
text: I didn’t realize my sugar was that high—I haven’t been checking regularly, but I have been feeling more thirsty and tired lately.

speaker: doctor
text: Given this new information, we need to assess whether you may have undiagnosed type 2 diabetes—and how it’s interacting with your hypertension management.

speaker: patient
text: Makes sense. My father was diagnosed with diabetes in his fifties, so I guess I’m at higher risk.

speaker: doctor
text: I just rechecked your heart rate—it’s now 111 bpm, still elevated but down from the earlier 130. That suggests some responsiveness, though we’ll monitor closely.

speaker: doctor
text: I’m going to add Atorvastatin 20mg at night to help with cardiovascular risk reduction, especially given your hypertension and now elevated glucose.

speaker: doctor
text: Interestingly, your repeat hemoglobin is now 147 g/L—lower than the 171 we saw earlier. We’ll want to rule out lab variability or possible early dehydration effect.

speaker: patient
text: I did drink quite a bit of water before coming in—maybe that affected it?

speaker: doctor
text: Possibly. We’ll repeat the CBC in a week under standardized conditions. In the meantime, let’s get you scheduled for HbA1c and fasting glucose testing tomorrow morning.

speaker: doctor
text: One more note—your temperature has dropped slightly to 36.0°C. It’s still within normal limits, but I’ll keep an eye on trends, especially with your symptoms.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, thank you. Should I hold off on the Lisinopril if my blood pressure feels low?

speaker: doctor
text: No—keep taking it as prescribed unless you develop dizziness or systolic BP below 100. We’ll adjust based on home readings and follow-up labs.

speaker: patient
text: Just to clarify—I remember your first reading said hemoglobin was 171 g/L and temperature was 36.9°C, so those numbers must be the baseline before things shifted.
